author: @douglasfell4199
text: Domestic solar panels have a problem  and that is that to grid tie solar you are capped at 3.6kw for single phase and 10.5kw for 3 phase. If your panels produce more than you use and this is more than the cap then this gets effectively dumped. Battery backup helps but this is costly and does not overcome the summer winter problem. Therefore rather than dumping or not using this electrical energy then can we use it to produce and medium term store hydrogen.

author: @walterceal7687
text:
I disagree with you on there needing to be an external power supply to charge the water to produce hydrogen and oxygen.

This process can be achieved through a toroidal field generator, my own model being built from only ring magnets and pvc electrical tape.

Even without the paramagnetic tubing as a coil, it still breaks down salt water from the interaction with the rotating magnetic currents.

Two or three of these toroidal field generators stacked up can not only break down salt water at a rate most people would confuse for boiling water but can be used as a high frequency AC generator, a long cycle AC generator if the main toroidal field is destabilized and if a stable toroidal is maintained, produces DC.

I plan to build a self regulating closed system in which the water is broken down by the magnetic field interactions, recombined by hydrogen fuel cells and the water returned to the tank.

Charged ammonia, shipped?  I can't say I prefer that option.🙂

author: @lowrads
text:
Decentralized production of dilute ammonia could be revolutionary for agriculture in fertigation applications.  We even have ammonia pipelines in the US following the Mississippi River valley.

The big problem with hydrogen is embrittlement of metals from hydride formation in the mineral matrix.  For this reason, I am a bit more interested in peroxo-group compounds.  Alkali metal peroxides will even absorb CO2 upon exposure to air, though that is incidental to their use as an energy storage medium.

author: @DannyWilliamH
text:
Doesn't sound preachy at all. The World needs energy solutions, clean and efficient energy solutions FAST. Even if we want to grant climate change deniers their point, it doesn't mean that humanity should continue to burn fossil fuels forever. We can't both literally and for the long-term health of the planet.

Therefore, if humans and our livestock already create millions of tons a day of ANYTHING that can help with this problem it needs to be made an option. ASAP. 

I would think that municipalities could somewhat easily separate-off some of the waste that comes in for basic filtering for this purpose. Some will have to go through to keep the basic waste filtering plants operational but you'd still have loads of waste available to be used for hydrogen production.

I don't know the numbers so I won't pretend to but if it turns out to be viable, you do it. No question.

On the goofier side, image a future where your toilet has an actual setting for #1 or #2. If you choose #1, it takes your waste and sends it to a local storage tank, as opposed to the normal route (many already have this).
Then, as your now hybrid electric car is plugged in and charging, it senses new waste reserves, stops charging the battery and starts charging the waste for hydrogen production. Efficient use of incoming power for storage and new energy production.

I can imagine a day where we grow up and it wouldn't be weird or silly at all.

Moving and storing Ammonia is in many ways safer.. far safer than Hydrogen. However leaks and spills will kill people. I then the dissolution of ammonia into Hydrogen and Nitrogen  using Hydrolysis is easy as you say however the problem then is the fuel cell technology that uses large amounts of rare earth (platinum group minerals) as catalysts to make fuel cells viable for the mass market.
Hydrogen in Natural gas (Methane) pipes is going to get a lot of people killed. However burning CO and CO2 in Hydrogen produces Carbon and water.  So Hydrogen can be used to reduce or remove CO2 from industrial processes that nessaccerily produce large amounts of CO2. For example the iron smelting and concrete industries.
Keep up the great output.

author: @paulharvey4403
text:
There used to be piss barrels on the streets, it was everyone duty to provide. The barrels of piss were taken to the leather tanneries in ships .   

Source wiki 
Taking the piss originates from the urine trade which was seen as an undesireable cargo for sailors working from the River Tyne. Because the city collected urine from public facilities and exported a refined version of it, it was often used as ship's ballast in place of water - having a resale value at the other end of the journey. Consequently, sailors discussing their cargo in local establishments would genially accuse others reputed to be lying about their cargo of "taking the piss" - or hauling urine.

time to bring back those ships..

author: @enyha
text:
Worked with industrial pressurised and bulk liquefied gasses for many years and ran my personal transport on LPG and briefly on CNG. Problem with with all gaseous fuels is energy density so on retiral I went for home produced bio-diesel and latterly to EVs . My future favored fuel must be methanol synthesised from recovered carbon dioxide as the carbon source in catalytic reactions with hydrogen, where the hydrogen is produced from water using renewable energy. Methanol is relaticely energy dense and works both with fuel cells and ICE/gas turbines, 
Methanol can be synthesised from carbon dioxide as the carbon source in catalytic reactions with hydrogen, where the hydrogen is produced from water using renewable energy. Key to success is finding a good catalyst so that methanol can be produced in high selectivity at an efficient rate. Formulations using precise atomic ratios of copper/palladium have shown significant improvement over previous methods.

author: @jjmcrosbie
text:
Thank you for another interesting and informative video.  Two questions:
1 - Are you aware of the safety requirements for atmospheres have a risk of Hydrogen content, ie Hydrogen flammable atmospheres?  These requirements are stricter than for petrol vapour, methane, carbon monoxide or explosive dust.  Full information from The British Approval Service for Electrical Equipment in Flammable Atmospheres.  BASEEFA, in the pretty hills near to Buxton, Derbyshire.  It was originally created for the coal mining industry.
Since my contacts with them in the late 1970's things have moved on, but at that time, there were a number of methods of achieving safety, but I don't plan to prolong this comment with that subject.
2 - I don't know how expensive it is to produce dead pure oxygen, but in the electrolysis of water it is produced.  In nascent form albeit.  It's dead pure but in highly aggressive nascent form (mono-atomic) rather than the less aggressive atmospheric di-atomic molecule O2.  This might offset the cost of the electrolysis.
Stay well.

author: @Cryptic_Triptych
text:
Here's an idea, how about you stop using the term "fossil fuels", you cannot make fuel from fossils. You should start by educating yourself on hydrocarbons, or synthetic blends. The hydrocarbons that everyone uses are made from algae. Bitumen (which has been used for more than 40,000 years) is made from the decomposition of algae into a sludge (plant extract oil) in aquatic conditions (using the weight of water to compress). Coal is a compressed version of this and Diamonds are even more so (using heat and pressure). 
The genius of this process of burning hydrocarbons is that water vapor and carbon dioxide are produced, and the trees love carbon and water, well everyone should love carbon and water because that is what we are made of, life and this planet would not exist without carbon and water. And so the circle is complete.

replies.text:

~Turned into(approx.) three paragraphs, seems like a little Inefficient.... 🤔
Me thinks that, perhaps you are extracting the urine?! 🙂
Andrea, Jasper and George the Pigeon. XxX.
Ps, Trees also Exhale carbon dioxide, usually during the night.
Algae, produce the majority of the oxygen, but, I am sorry, I'm not up to giving biology or ecology lessons just now...
We wouldn't be having an ecological crisis, if we followed your "logic"!!

replies.author: @JA-ti3wd
replies.text: I'm not clear on whether this post is intended as a joke but I'm going to assume it's real. No one thinks you get hydrocarbons from fossils. It's a term to quickly convey that these hydrocarbons left the carbon cycle millions of years ago. The problem with adding that carbon back into the carbon cycle is that it adds too much CO2 for the existing plants to take out of the atmosphere and therefore the CO2 levels rise, trap heat within the atmosphere and therefore adds energy to the system. In the main that means higher temperatures but it also means more variation in weather, more frequent extremes and greater extremes. Even if we switched to a purely hydrogen economy tomorrow we'll still have crops, there will still be animals around - i.e. there will still be a carbon cycle. We just wouldn't be making things worse through using hydrocarbons for most of the energy we consume (ignoring our food).

text: Historical ignorance is sad, seeing we used to collect pee as alum substitute in dye-fixing etc... I'm disappointed that at this stage we're not having a full and frank discussion over *every* use of fossil fuels and their by-products in modern society -- from fertilizers to brewing, to plastics, to silicones, to farming, etc. People need to know what alternatives there are if any, discuss what we're willing to still use, and how long it will take to bring those alternatives to market. It's also disappointing that the 'greening' of the UK is openly driven by a want to punish us for past CO2 production where we're domestically producing 1% of the world's CO2 emissions (not allowing for CO2 of imports).

author: @1967spark
text:
Given that hydrogen isn't readily available naturally, I prefer to consider hydrogen as being more like a battery than a true power source. i.e. to create the hydrogen you need to input energy and of course the energy you extract is going to be less than that used to produce the hydrogen. 

Yes there may be efficiencies using ammonia compared to water, but ammonia in substantial quantities also requires careful handling (it can be explosive too).
Of course when dissolved in water it is fairly safe, but that will reduce the efficiencies when extracting the hydrogen. 

However we should also be looking at using natural sources of ammonia (e.g. urine ) instead of manufacturing it for purposes such as fertilisers etc. Most ammonia is created using fossil fuels.

author: @donaldhanson2908
text:
Great idea. 
But... (you knew that was coming right?)
One of the problems the managers of waste water have is the "contaminates" in the waste stream.
Human urine in modern societies is a more of a soup of all the chemicals we imbibe. Estrogens and Antidepressants being particularly troublesome. Heart and diabetes medications. And all the over the counter medications, dietary supplements...
They all persist in the effluent of waste water treatment facilities.
I too like the Idea of recovering energy from waste a great deal. 
That includes recovering methane from raw sewage and land fills.

author: @reincarnatethylacine70
text: Ammonia has a cost advantage in that the existing LPG infrastructure can be easily adapted (removing copper and brass fittings and replacing with steel). There's x4 times the hydrogen stored in a cubic metre of Ammonia than in a cubic metre of solid hydrogen. Over a decade ago a small team from the University of Idaho patented a method of producing Ammonia using a low energy biologically based system. It was touted as being an ideal solution for stranded (isolated) green energy producers by enabling them to store and ship the energy direct to the end user. The economics were said to make on-farm Ammonia production viable for direct use as fertilizer. The project failed to attract commercial backing and after several years the patents were acquired by Seimans who has yet do do anything with them.

replies.author: @patomahony9747
replies.text:
Reading comments I fear this is a long way off. Powerful vested interests and their lobbying will prevent this. You say how could this be possible. Look at prohibition in USA just after world war 1. Fossil fuel magnates had invested heavily in oil etc. They were not about to let joe soap run his model t on alcohol so lobbied government to put a stop to it. Similar happened in late 1990s to early 2000s with dual fuel cars. Ecu programmed to run good on petrol but run badly on ethanol. Not sure what happened to bio diesel world wide but here in Ireland like ethanol the government taxed it to death. 
Ran a diesel (vw transporter with old school mechanical injectors) on recycled chipper oil for years, no special mods. Just triple filtered the waste chipper oil. In warm weather ran 70% oil to 30% diesel. In winter went with a 50/50 mix. Just add the oil to tank with engine running to get it mixed. Fitted a second fuel filter and later on a warming jacket to these filters to aid starting in winter. 
Sadly not really possible on modern engines because the plastic polymers used melt on contact with biodiesel. 
Currently it’s illegal here to gather and store rainwater. Put up any kind of wind generator no matter how small. 
Look at fine print in car insurance policies. Hydrogen powered vehicles are not covered. 
So we must save our planet but powers that be think. Tax tax. 
Next we will be told we can’t spend a penny lol
Rant over

replies.text:
@David_Mash the simple diy option would probably be a low volt pv/earth battery electrolysis to trompe/or water sealed low pressure gasometer bubbler using a high column of water to keep the trompe psi up & cool the gas.
for safety put it all in a simple deep fake well to keep it contained in any accident after you make & test its output to work time use per on demand job/device.

author: @scantrain5007
text:
Dear Rob,
a few months ago you been mentioned to use urea for hydrogen production. If it's possible with urin it's much better as to look after urea because as you said: Urin is easy fo find and collect. (Farmers do that every single day...) 
 
 The talk against hydrogen production was always the low efiency. But in my mind it should be possible to solve the energy problem with solar cells. 

 Many owner of solar pannels have in the summer to much electric energy and in the winter not enough. So why not produse hydrogen in the summer and store it for using in the winter? 
Off course - such a system is not easy to create and establish... But much more simple and cheaper as a nuclear power station or pressing oil and gas out off the ground.

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: Storing hydrogen as a gas is a problem as the smallest molecule it likes to get out and it weakens the container material to do it.

replies.author: @paulmaydaynight9925
replies.text:
@Barskor1 it's not so much the smaller partial size although that doesn't help, it's energy under internal pressure/heat compared to outside, as in higher 'energy' will always try to equalise in to any lower energy area,
 hence equal/higher external pressure such as a simple deep water column around its container, a double pressurised container &/or super cooling any gas will change it's energetic gas state in to/near liquid calmer cool state

replies.text:
@DavidPaulNewtonScott My friends Dad was a keen gardener, and used to make male visitors pee outside in an old rain barrel..
Leaving it to "mellow" for a good six months,,, during the summer barbecues, it was a fight to get an Upwind position to eat.... 🙄💞😝

author: @karlmyers6518
text: I like hydrogen as a product if we can get it via greener methods.

replies.author: @BoB4jjjjs
replies.text:
To get the amount of electric we are going to need for cars in the future is going to lead to a lot of pollution, there is no way is Wind and hydro power going to generate enough electric.  Electric is not a easy product to store.  Plus all the batteries put in cars, the waste products are very toxic, plus just making the batteries produces a lot of toxic waste, so electric cars are not so green as you might think.
However, Hydrogen cars and run our engines of today with a bit of modification without all the pollution from making and disposing of batteries for electric cars.  There are a few problems to get over with Hydrogen, but buses are running on it right now.  We can make Hydrogen at night when less power is required to be used during the day.  I like the idea of Hydrogen rather than electric cars.  We should be able to fuel up and go with Hydrogen in the future rather than sit at a recharging station for a minimum of 30 minutes (which will damage the batteries if used a lot like most will) but with Hydrogen we should be able to fuel and go in the future.
It will also take the worry of where you are going to get a charging station that fits your car (they are not all the same) and the stress of "Am I going to make it home?"
I think the future, or at least I hope the future is Hydrogen and not electric, though I think both will have a place, Electric will dominate town driving while Hydrogen will be for long distance and the countryside.
At least that is how I see the future, if there is one!
I don't much care for getting Hydrogen from fossil fuels though.
Water and pee is a good way to go.  :-))

replies.author: @LoremIpsum1970
replies.text: @BoB4jjjjs ...add the additional mining, rise in prices and ecological damage caused extracting the minerals for batteries -- though it is hoped that Li batteries will be reused so usage will be self-sustaining not requiring more mining ... I'd like to see it happen though...

author: @Stephen-dg9de
text: Does urine require electrolytes for current to freely flow as water requires sodium potassium.

replies.author: @chuckles1808
replies.text: Urine is packed full of electrolytes already, so it's prepped for electrolysis.
